content.text: Android ML Kit ships several ready-to-use NLU models that run entirely on-device: language identification, entity extraction (dates, addresses, phone numbers, ISBNs), smart reply suggestions, and text classification. These models are fast, private, and offline-capable. But they have fixed vocabularies, narrow domains, and no reasoning ability. Claude covers what they cannot — ambiguous intent, multi-turn dialogue, structured extraction from complex prose, and open-domain Q&A. Combining both gives you the best of each: ML Kit handles clear-cut cases instantly, Claude handles the hard ones when connectivity is available.

The Claude API key must never appear in the Android binary, resources, or on-device storage. Even hardware-backed Keystore cannot protect a secret that belongs server-side. All Claude calls go through your backend proxy, which holds the API key and authenticates clients with short-lived session tokens. ML Kit models run fully on-device and need no credentials.

attrs.alt: Routing architecture: user text goes through ML Kit NLU; high-confidence results return immediately; low-confidence queries route to backend proxy then Claude API
attrs.title: On-Device NLU + Claude Cloud Fallback — Routing Architecture

content.text: The routing logic runs on the device. ML Kit processes every input first. If the model returns a high-confidence result and the device is offline, you return the on-device answer. If confidence is below your threshold or the query is structurally complex (long, multi-clause, contains a question mark with no named entity), you route to the backend proxy, which calls Claude and streams the response back.
ML Kit's entity extractor recognizes structured data — dates, addresses, phone numbers, ISBNs, flight numbers, monetary amounts — without a network call. You annotate text and receive typed
The entity extractor model is downloaded once and cached. Call
at app startup or when the feature first loads — not in the hot path of each request. The model file is roughly 1 MB for English.

The router runs ML Kit synchronously in-process, inspects the results, and decides whether to return them or escalate to Claude. The decision uses three signals: entity confidence, query complexity (word count and question structure), and network availability.
, the app sends the query to your backend, which holds the Claude API key and calls the Messages API. The response streams back as SSE. On Android, OkHttp's
or a simple chunked-body reader converts the stream into a
ViewModel: Wiring the Router to UI
The ViewModel owns both the router and the proxy client. It processes each user message, consults the router, and emits state updates the Composable observes.

If your app serves multilingual users, run ML Kit's language identification first. A text in Spanish with no English entity model match will always produce low confidence — not because the query is complex, but because the model does not cover that language. Detect the language and either load the appropriate ML Kit model or route directly to Claude for languages your on-device models do not support.
immediately without invoking the entity extractor.
Queries that need Claude but arrive while the device is offline can be queued for later processing. Use a
database as the queue and WorkManager to drain it when connectivity is restored.
delays the worker until the device is online.
prevents duplicate drain workers from being enqueued if connectivity changes fire multiple times.
Entity extractor and language ID models download on first use unless you call
eagerly. On a cold start the first request either blocks for a download or fails silently with an empty result set. Download during app initialization or in a background prefetch.
Using confidence as the only routing signal.

ML Kit may return a high-confidence date entity for "Meet me tomorrow" but Claude is still better suited when the surrounding intent ("...so we can discuss my refund") requires reasoning. Combine confidence with word count, turn count, and question presence.
The session token is the credential for your backend proxy. Never log it at
or include it in error messages that surface to the UI. Restrict its scope to the retrofit/okhttp layer and zero it from memory after use.

If the pending queue stores health, financial, or personal queries in a Room database, encrypt it. Use SQLCipher or pass-phrase encrypt the Room DB. Unencrypted queue entries are readable by anyone with USB debugging access or a backup extraction.

On some low-storage devices the ML Kit model may not download successfully. Wrap entity extraction in a try-catch and fall back to routing every query to Claude rather than crashing. The hybrid system degrades to cloud-only, which is still correct behavior.
ML Kit entity extraction, language ID, and smart reply run fully on-device — no latency, no cost, no credentials required.
The routing decision combines ML Kit confidence, word count, turn count, and connectivity; low-confidence or complex queries escalate to Claude via a backend proxy.
The Claude API key lives server-side only; the Android app authenticates to the proxy with a short-lived session token stored in the Android Keystore.
Language identification gates the entity extractor: unsupported languages route directly to Claude rather than producing empty or misleading low-confidence results.
Offline queries that need Claude are stored in a Room queue and drained by WorkManager when connectivity is restored; sensitive queues should use SQLCipher encryption.
